claim_summary: Adopting expansive 'power poses' for two minutes reduces cortisol and increases confidence before stressful situations.
original_citation: Carney DR, Cuddy AJC, Yap AJ (2010). Power posing: Brief nonverbal displays affect neuroendocrine levels and risk tolerance. Psychological Science, 21(10), 1363-1368.
detail: Primary author Carney (2016) stated she does not believe power pose effects are real; large multi-lab replication (Simmons & Simonsohn, 2020) found no meaningful effect. Do not ship as anxiety/courage evidence.

claim_summary: Willpower is a finite resource that depletes with use — ego depletion — explaining why people fail at self-control late in the day.
original_citation: Baumeister RF, et al. (1998). Ego depletion: Is the active self a limited resource? Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 74(5), 1252-1265.
detail: Multi-lab Registered Replication Report (Hagger et al., 2016) failed to replicate core ego depletion effect. Concept still popular in pop psych but robustness rating weakened — excluded from bank.

claim_summary: Delay of gratification at age four strongly predicts lifelong success, income, and emotional health.
original_citation: Mischel W, Shoda Y, Rodriguez ML (1989). Delay of gratification in children. Science, 244(4907), 933-938.
detail: Watts, Duncan & Quan (2018) reanalysis with controls for family background reduced effect to near zero. Marshmallow-test-as-destiny framing rejected; do not use for financial or anxiety chapters.

claim_summary: Listening to Mozart temporarily raises IQ and reduces anxiety.
original_citation: Rauscher FH, Shaw GL, Ky KN (1993). Music and spatial task performance. Nature, 365(6447), 611.
detail: Original study was small, specific to spatial tasks, short-lived. Subsequent meta-analyses find no durable cognitive or anxiety benefit from Mozart exposure alone.

claim_summary: Repeating positive affirmations ('I am confident') reliably reduces anxiety for everyone.
original_citation: Wood JV, Perunovic WQE, Lee JW (2009). Positive self-statements: Power for some, peril for others. Psychological Science, 20(7), 860-866.
detail: Affirmations helped low-self-esteem participants less or backfired. Cannot ship as universal TAKEAWAY without caveat — bank uses self-compassion and graded exposure instead.

claim_summary: The third Monday in January is the most depressing day of the year.
original_citation: Arnall D (2005). Press release commissioned by Sky Travel (not peer-reviewed).
detail: No underlying peer-reviewed study. Formula was marketing construct. Never cite as evidence.

claim_summary: Humans use only 10% of their brain; unlocking the rest would end anxiety/overthinking.
original_citation: Popular myth; debunked in Jarrett C (2014). Great Myths of the Brain. Wiley-Blackwell.
detail: Neuroimaging shows widespread brain use. No primary study supports 10% claim.

claim_summary: Trauma is stored in every cell and must be released through specific body techniques or it remains forever.
original_citation: Various pop-somatic sources without controlled trial support.
detail: Trauma affects body and brain (robust). 'Stored in every cell' / mandatory release protocol is not evidence-backed at robust level. Use van der Kolk/LeDoux with appropriate robustness only.

claim_summary: A 20-second hug reliably cures anxiety by flooding oxytocin.
original_citation: Popular press extrapolation from small oxytocin/hug studies.
detail: Oxytocin response to touch varies by relationship context and study design. Do not ship as universal anxiety intervention.

claim_summary: People learn best when teaching matches their VARK learning style (visual/auditory/etc.).
original_citation: Fleming ND, Mills C (1992). VARK questionnaire.
detail: Pashler et al. (2008) and subsequent reviews find no evidence that matching instruction to learning style improves outcomes. Excluded from evidence bank.

claim_summary: Depression is simply a chemical imbalance corrected by medication.
original_citation: Popular pharmaceutical marketing simplification; Moncrieff J, et al. (2022) systematic review on serotonin and depression.
detail: Serotonin model oversimplified; depression is multifactorial. Bank uses state-vs-identity and behavioral activation without single-cause chemical claims.

claim_summary: Everyone grieving must pass through five linear stages: denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance.
original_citation: Kubler-Ross E (1969). On Death and Dying. New York: Macmillan.
detail: Kubler-Ross model descriptive not prescriptive; grief is non-linear. Dual-process and continuing-bonds models used instead.

claim_summary: Sugar intake causes ADHD symptoms in children and adults.
original_citation: Popular myth; Wolraich ML, et al. (2011) meta-analysis found no significant effect of sugar on behavior.
detail: Sugar-hyperactivity link not supported at population level. Excluded to avoid misinformation.

claim_summary: Addiction is cured by willpower alone if the person wants it badly enough.
original_citation: Popular moral model; contradicted by ASAM disease model and MAT trial literature.
detail: Willpower-only framing increases shame and delays treatment. Bank uses disease-plus-recovery-support framing.

claim_summary: Repeating 'I am beautiful' daily reliably cures body dysmorphia for everyone.
original_citation: Wood JV, et al. (2009) positive self-statements; body image intervention literature.
detail: Appearance-only affirmations can backfire; functionality-appreciation and media-literacy approaches have stronger trial support.
